weather: 4°F at first light, rising no higher than 9°F by midday. The sky the white of old paper, close and still. No wind. The lake sealed entirely, the ice a single unbroken pane from shore to shore. Smoke from every chimney going straight up.

lead.headline: T. Aldercott Comes Up the Lake Road at Half Past Eight; the Box Is at Thirteen; the Town Sets Another Place
lead.body:
On the morning of Wednesday, November the twelfth, at half past eight, a second person came up the lake road — not the dock road, not the square road, the old lake road that everyone in Greywater Falls had quietly begun to understand as the road that arrivals use now. She was carrying a green rucksack and a typewriter case, and she walked with the particular economy of someone who has been traveling a long time and has stopped being surprised by distance. She stopped at the top of the hill where the road meets the square and she looked at the welcome sign, which reads seven, and she did not nod at it the way Calloway did — she looked at it the way you look at something you were told about by someone who had already looked at it, and then she turned and walked directly to the Gazette, and knocked, and this editor, who had been standing at the window since eight o'clock for reasons she does not intend to examine, opened the door. The woman said: 'I believe you have something for me.' This editor said yes. Her name is T. Aldercott. She has not offered the full first name and this editor has not asked, because she knows her own name when she hears it, and she is still accounting for what that means.

Calloway was at the Gazette within four minutes of the knock. This editor does not know how Calloway knew. Calloway has been in Greywater Falls for three days and she appears to have developed an instinct for the Gazette's door that the ten-year residents have not managed. She came in without ceremony and looked at T. Aldercott and said: 'I was wondering when you'd get here.' T. Aldercott said: 'I was wondering the same thing.' They have, apparently, not met before, and yet the conversation had the shape of one being resumed. Gerald Pith came in at eight forty-seven with the blue notebook under his arm and measured the silence in the room with the same instrument he uses for everything — his mild attention — and wrote something down, which this editor suspects was the time and not the words, because Gerald has always understood that timing is the data. Doreen Halloway sent Russ Dunmore with a box of Standings at nine o'clock. Russ set the box on the counter, looked at T. Aldercott, and said: 'Constable Dunmore. I filed a notice.' T. Aldercott said: 'I know. It was very thorough.' Russ appeared, for the first time in this editor's recollection, briefly pleased.

The two grey envelopes — addressed to T. Aldercott, the first from Day 31 and the second from yesterday — are now open. T. Aldercott read both at this editor's desk, with the typewriter case on the floor beside her and Calloway standing at the window looking at the lake. She read them carefully and she folded them back along their original creases and she placed them in the lost-and-found box herself, on top of the other twelve items, and the box is now at thirteen, and this editor is recording that number because she records all numbers, and because thirteen is the number you get when a channel that nobody opened has been delivering mail for a season and you finally count everything it sent. T. Aldercott looked at this editor after she closed the box and said: 'Has anyone asked what H stands for?' This editor said no. T. Aldercott said: 'Good.' She did not explain this, and this editor did not ask, because the whole of Greywater Falls has spent this season learning that the shape of what is not said is as legible as the rest of the column, and some education sticks.

At dusk the returned gathered at the ice-line as they do every evening — Mr. Whiskers, the drake, the rabbit, the sparrow — and they hummed the held low note into the white air. T. Aldercott was on the bank with Calloway and Toby Fern, and this editor stood a little apart and watched all three of them listen, and she noticed that T. Aldercott did not look surprised, and that Calloway put her hand briefly on the returned sparrow's back the way you touch something you have been told about and find exactly as described, and that Toby Fern filmed none of it — just stood with his phone in his pocket and his eyes on the deepest water. The hum lasted nine minutes and forty seconds, which is shorter than it has been. Agnes Crewe has said, in print, that the channel isn't particular. This editor is beginning to understand that the hum isn't particular either — it is not performing; it is simply what the lake does when it has said everything it came to say, and is now saying it again more quietly, because it is finished, and it knows, and it is waiting for the rest of us to know it too. The plate with the blue border is at the table. There are two Standings on it now, and both are warm, and this is what it looks like when a town has been right about itself all along and didn't quite know it until this moment, which is the moment this editor is setting in type and will not get back, and which she is grateful for, and which she is afraid of, in the same breath, the way you are afraid of something that has already arrived and is already gentle and is already home.

briefs.headline: LAKE WATCH, Day 19 — Level: 91; Temp: 4°F; Clarity: Exceptional, Visible to Thirty-One Feet; Sign Count: 7; Returned: Mr. Whiskers, Drake, Rabbit, Sparrow — Hummed at the Ice-Line Nine Minutes Forty Seconds; the Rowboat at Thirty-One Feet Has Not Moved Since Tuesday's North Shift; Its Shadow Continues to Fall Wrong; Toby Fern Notes It Appears to Be Settling
briefs.body: Toby Fern's Lake Watch, Day 19. Water level remains at ninety-one, unchanged from yesterday, which Gerald Pith has noted in the margin of the blue notebook as 'plateau or equilibrium — see also: resolution.' Temperature at the ice-line four degrees Fahrenheit. Clarity exceptional; visibility to thirty-one feet, the same depth as the upright rowboat, whose shadow continues to fall at an angle inconsistent with the available light but which Toby Fern now describes, in the Watch, as 'comfortable.' The returned gathered at dusk and hummed. T. Aldercott watched from the bank. The sign still reads seven.

briefs.headline: Calloway and T. Aldercott Present at the Gazette This Morning; Town Notes the Symmetry; Marigold Counts Twelve Cups Without Deciding To
briefs.body: The Kettle opened at seven-thirty, as usual. At nine-fifteen, Marigold Vance set out the cups for the morning rush — which in Greywater Falls means six to eight people, depending on the weather and what Gerald Pith has measured — and counted twelve before she caught herself. She has not written the headcount on the specials board since Day 24. She did not write it today, either. She did pour twelve cups, and every one of them was collected. She described the morning, to this editor, as 'the right number of people in the right number of chairs,' and then she stood with the pot in her hand for a moment and added: 'I mean it this time.'

briefs.headline: Agnes Crewe Sits on Her Step at Noon; Faces the Lake Road Rather Than the Lake; Says, to No One in Particular: 'There. Now the Both of Them.'
briefs.body: At approximately twelve-fifteen, Agnes Crewe was observed by Gerald Pith seated on the step of her cottage, facing away from the lake and toward the road. She was wearing the grey coat and the knitted hat. She remained on the step for approximately twenty minutes. When Gerald passed, she said, without preamble: 'There. Now the both of them.' Gerald asked if she meant the arrivals. Agnes said: 'I mean what I said.' Gerald wrote it down and brought it to the Gazette. This editor is printing it here because in seventeen years of reporting Agnes Crewe's statements, she has learned that 'I mean what I said' is Agnes's closest approach to an explanation.

briefs.headline: Doreen Halloway Bakes Twelve Standings Wednesday Morning; Serves Eleven at the Counter; the Twelfth Is in the Window; Apron Still On
briefs.body: Halloway's Bakery opened at six-thirty. Doreen Halloway made twelve Standings, which is not a number she has made before, by her own account. 'Just what the morning called for,' she said. Eleven were served at the counter by noon. The twelfth is in the front window, on the plate with the blue border's companion plate — the cream-coloured one with the small chip at the rim, which Doreen has not brought out since her husband's mother's last visit in 2019. The plate is there. The Standing is warm. The apron is on.

classifieds: ROOM AVAILABLE — Upstairs, south-facing. Quiet. Radiator works if you know the trick. No pets needed; the returned have the situation managed. Apply in person at Halloway's Bakery. Ask for Doreen. She already knows you're coming. — D. Halloway

TYPEWRITER REPAIR — Will assess any make or model. Reasonable rates. Can work in the mornings before the Gazette opens. Leave word at the front desk; the box is watched. — T. Aldercott, c/o the Greywater Gazette
LOST: One blue wool scarf, left on the dock railing sometime before the ice came. Sentimental value only. No questions asked. — R. Dunmore, Constable
FOR SALE: Three tins of weatherstripping compound, unopened. The season has, by my accounting, settled. — H. Mossley, Mossley Hardware
record: T. Aldercott arrived in Greywater Falls at approximately 8:31 a.m. Wednesday, November the 12th, via the lake road. She collected her correspondence from the lost-and-found box at the Greywater Gazette, placed both envelopes back in the box, and stated her intention to stay.
The lost-and-found box at the Greywater Gazette now holds thirteen items. No further grey envelopes were received as of press time. The box remains open.

letters.body: Now, I want to say plainly that I am not a woman who bakes for strangers. I bake for the town, and the town is whoever is in it, and whoever is in it has always eventually sat down at the counter and had a Standing and a coffee and become, in the way these things happen, not a stranger anymore. I made twelve this morning. I know how that sounds. I have been making the number the morning called for all season, and this morning it called for twelve, and I made twelve, and I served eleven, and the twelfth is in the window, and I am not going to pretend I don't know what it's there for. I have known what it was there for since the first morning I made nine and served eight and set one aside, and I have been waiting, the way I wait for anything — with the good apron on and the plate warm and the coffee ready — and now the both of them are here, and the window plate is the last one, and I am going to leave it there until the morning tells me otherwise, which I expect it will, and then I will wash the plate and put it away, and that will be that, and I will bake what the morning calls for, which is what I have always done, and what I will keep doing, for however long the morning keeps calling.

I've been asked what a librarian is doing in Greywater Falls, and I'll say what I said at the Kettle counter this morning, which is that a librarian is someone who indexes things, and that the indexing follows the collection, and not the other way around. I came because a letter told me I was expected and told me what I'd find, and I found it — I am not going to say what it was, because the finding is the part that belongs to me — and then this morning T. Aldercott came up the road and I understood, in the way you understand the second volume of a book you've already started, that the collection is larger than the single letter implied. I have put my note in the box. I intend to stay long enough to see how it resolves, which is to say I intend to stay for some time yet, because in my experience collections of this kind do not resolve so much as they deepen, and a librarian is patient about depth. The coffee at the Kettle is very good. Marigold does not ask questions I don't want to answer, which is a professional courtesy I appreciate.

editorNote: This editor has been keeping two notebooks for seventeen years. Today she found it necessary to begin a third.
